title: New Foremen Onboarding
body: When a new foreman is thrown straight onto an independent shift without structured preparation, you get the exact supervision-quality failures that show up repeatedly in your NCR corpus — supervisor present, didn't intervene. Four weeks of supervised practice fixes that gap; a one-day induction doesn't.
keyPoints: The program runs four pillars — technical competence, safety and compliance leadership, shift control and decision-making, and people leadership. Assessment at Week 4 rates each pillar 1–5. A foreman who scores well on technical but poorly on leadership isn't ready.

Week 2 is where the field skills get built: match-up, spotting, road standards, hazard ID, and NCR writing under supervision. This is also where most new foremen expose gaps they didn't know they had.

The Week 4 readiness decision has three outcomes — Ready, Ready with Support, or Not Yet Ready. "Not Yet Ready" needs a defined path: what additional training, who signs off the second readiness call, and how long the extension runs. That path should be explicit before the program starts, not figured out when someone fails.

The 90-day post-onboarding support (weekly check-ins with the Superintendent, monthly KPI review, peer mentoring) is where the program actually cements. Week 4 is the test — the 90 days are where the standard gets held.
apply: If you have a foreman currently in their first 90 days post-onboarding, their shift handover quality is the single clearest indicator of whether the onboarding actually landed — check it specifically at shift start and shift end, where your NCR data says incidents cluster.
